title: Suspended Per Section 3: The Rule That Must Name Itself, and the Money With a Clock
setting:
Petra Lindqvist has sold refurbished audio equipment on Kestrel Marketplace for eleven years — turntables rescued from house clearances, amplifiers rebuilt at her kitchen bench — and Kestrel is forty percent of her income. On a Tuesday morning her account is suspended: 'Listings removed for policy violation (inauthentic items) — see Section 3.' Her balance, £9,180, is frozen; the notice does not say for how long, or by whom, or what in Section 3 she is supposed to have breached. The appeal asks her to submit invoices proving the provenance of goods bought at estate sales, which is to say it asks for documents that have never existed. Around her, an industry has grown to serve exactly this moment: consultants who charge sellers to draft 'plans of action' — ritual confessions to violations they may not have committed, because contrition parses better than innocence in the machine that reads them. And there is a detail Petra does not yet know: Section 3 changed three weeks ago. Her listings complied with the rule that existed when she wrote them.

Every element is documented. The Verge's 'Prime and Punishment' mapped the suspension economy on Amazon's marketplace: sellers ambushed by unexplained enforcement, funds held, reinstatement by confession, a private judiciary with no published law. The EU found the pattern so routine it legislated against it — the Platform-to-Business Regulation (2019/1150) obliges platforms to give a statement of reasons for restriction, suspension or termination, advance notice of terms changes, and an internal complaint process, precisely because none of this was standard. And the CMA's mobile ecosystems study documented the dependence that gives the wrong its weight: for millions of small firms the platform is not a counterparty but the marketplace itself, and delisting is not a contract dispute — it is exile.

Kestrel is fiction, piloting under a seller-council settlement what the regulation demands and the corpus already had words for: rules that change only on notice with an expiry, adverse decisions that cite the rule version and the facts, money held only by an act with an author and a deadline. The walkthrough tests the two moves the gig case did not need: the amendment ladder pointed at terms-of-service, and a funds freeze given a clock — because for a seller the account is the shop, but the balance is the wages.

precedents.event: 'Prime and Punishment': the seller-suspension economy — unexplained enforcement, frozen funds, reinstatement by ritual confession
precedents.shows: Documented the private judiciary this scenario replays: sellers suspended with no usable reasons, balances held, and an appeals culture in which paid consultants draft confessions to unproven violations because admitting fault parses better than contesting it — process without law, and livelihoods inside it.

precedents.event: Platform-to-Business Regulation (EU) 2019/1150: statement of reasons, notice of terms changes, internal complaint handling
precedents.shows: The legislature converging on the corpus's discipline because platforms would not adopt it voluntarily: restriction, suspension or termination of a business user must come with a statement of reasons; terms changes need advance notice; complaints need a real process — the demand this scenario turns from obligation into schema.

precedents.event: CMA mobile ecosystems market study: the dependence that makes delisting exile
precedents.shows: The regulator's account of why the wrong weighs what it does: platform gatekeepers set and enforce the terms of access to entire markets, small counterparties have no practical alternative, and the rules' application is opaque — dependence documented at ecosystem scale, the backdrop to every unexplained suspension.

community.name: Kestrel Marketplace — seller-decisions log (P2B pilot under seller-council settlement)

walkthrough.actor: Petra Lindqvist, and the sentence with a section number
walkthrough.narrative: The backdrop, as documented: a suspension that cites 'Section 3' the way a wall cites gravity; £9,180 frozen by nobody, for no stated period; an appeal that wants invoices estate sales never issued; and the reinstatement consultants, the truest symptom — an industry that exists because confessing to a violation you did not commit is the rational move when the tribunal is a parser and the law is unpublished. The Verge mapped this economy years ago. The EU legislated against it. What neither could reach is the detail that turns process into vertigo: the rule Petra is accused under is not the rule she listed under, and nothing in the old world would ever have told her.

walkthrough.actor: Kestrel, putting its rulebook on the ladder
walkthrough.narrative: Under the settlement, Kestrel's terms stop being weather and become amendments. Section 3's new used-goods provenance requirement arrives as a rules.amendment entry: the diff committed by hash, notice served fifteen days before effect — the regulation's own minimum — an effective date, and an expiry two years out unless renewed. This is §5 pointed at a terms-of-service: the rules can change, in stages, with expiry dates, and every version is a dated public object. Nothing stops Kestrel writing a harsher Section 3. What stops is the rule that arrives already in force, retroactively, invisibly — because from this entry on, every listing and every enforcement carries the version it happened under.

body.authorizing_rule: kestrel-seller-settlement/2026 §2 (terms change on 15 days' notice, versioned, with expiry)
body.note: The rule change as a first-class act: noticed, dated, diffed, expiring. A term nobody re-justifies in two years lapses — the amendment ladder's discipline, transplanted to a marketplace.

walkthrough.actor: The suspension, made to name itself
walkthrough.narrative: The flag fires against Petra's listings anyway — and now it must say what it is. The denial-trace carries the rule version (v12), the specific listings, the specific defect claimed (provenance documentation absent), the creation dates of the listings themselves, and an appeal path; the detector's working detail is sealed but committed, readable by Petra and an authorised auditor. One line in the entry does the work of the whole reform: the listings are dated 2025, the rule is dated June 2026. In the old world that mismatch lived in Petra's memory, unprovable; here it is two fields of the same public record, and anyone — Petra, the council, the regulator — can subtract one date from the other.

body.note: The statement of reasons the regulation demands, as a validity condition: rule version, named defect, named listings, their dates beside the rule's. The version mismatch is now arithmetic on the entry's own face.

walkthrough.actor: The freeze, given an author and a deadline
walkthrough.narrative: One minute later the money is held — as its own act, because the settlement forbids a freeze to travel silently inside a suspension. The hold names its rule, its amount, and its release clock: sixty days maximum pending investigation, and a hold past its clock is not a lapse but a visible breach the witnesses count. The FOI lesson, transplanted to an escrow balance: what the old world made a formless waiting — money gone, nobody accountable, no date to point at — is now a specific act by a specific authority that expires on a specific day. £9,180 is still frozen this morning. But it is frozen *by someone, under something, until a date*, and each of those three facts is one the old notice never contained.

body.note: The freeze as an act with an author and a clock. Silence past release_due is itself a breach on the record — the slow-walk foreclosed for money the way the corpus forecloses it for information.

walkthrough.actor: Petra Lindqvist, disputing with dates instead of contrition
walkthrough.narrative: Petra's dispute is one paragraph and no remorse. She cites the suspension entry against the amendment entry: the listings predate v12's effective date; v11 contained no provenance requirement; the settlement's own terms bar retroactive enforcement. There is no plan of action, no consultant, no confession engineered for a parser — the two entries argue with each other, and she merely points. The reinstatement economy the Verge documented existed because disputes had no material to work with except the seller's posture. Give the dispute two dated public facts and the posture industry has nothing left to sell.

walkthrough.actor: The review, and the money's early release
walkthrough.narrative: Day eight: the human review upholds her. The finding writes itself from the record — listings created under v11, v12 not retroactive, suspension misapplied — and the consequences execute along the entries' own references: listings restored, the hold released fifty-one days before its clock, and a misapplication counter incremented in the quarter's aggregate. The reviewer spent eleven minutes, and that number lands on the record too. Petra's case was easy precisely because the reform made it easy: the wrongness of her suspension was computable from two fields. The harder cases — where the rule was in force and the judgment is real — remain judgment, and the log's only promise there is that judgment must sign its work.

body.finding: listings predate kestrel-terms/v12 effective date; v11 imposed no provenance requirement; enforcement misapplied a non-retroactive amendment
body.note: The outcome executes along the record's own references — listings, hold, aggregate — and the review signs its reasoning, its time, and its role.

walkthrough.actor: The quarter, counting the machine's own misfires
walkthrough.narrative: The Q2 aggregate publishes what no seller could previously prove and no regulator could cheaply discover: 4,102 suspensions; twelve percent citing a rule version newer than the listings they struck — the retroactivity rate, now a named number; median hold thirty-one days; 3.2 percent of holds past their clocks, each a counted breach; twenty-seven percent of disputed suspensions reversed. The regulation's enforcement problem has always been that its breaches are invisible one seller at a time — pattern_of_lawful_acts, the corpus's oldest signature. A witnessed quarterly table is the pattern pre-assembled: the P2B case against a non-compliant platform, written by the platform's own log.

body.note: Anonymous aggregate: the retroactivity rate, the money's median winter, and the breach count — the regulation's evidence base, standing and witnessed instead of subpoenaed.

walkthrough.actor: The rulebook's author, still the counterparty
walkthrough.narrative: The honest ceiling, and it is structural. Kestrel still writes Section 3. Version 13 can lawfully require provenance papers estate sales will never produce, and if it arrives on proper notice with a clean diff and a dated expiry, the log will record the extinction of Petra's business model in perfect order — the kernel makes rule-by-decree legible, slower, and dated; it does not make it fair, and whether a term is fair is company law and the regulation's substance, refused here on principle (refusal 11). Adoption is the second ceiling: the P2B binds on paper across the EU, but its enforcement runs through national courts seller by seller, and a platform off the log offers exactly what the Verge found — process without law. The settlement Kestrel signed is what the corpus's other platform case called being marched to the record by statute; the march is real, and it is slow, and the sellers' dependence the CMA documented does not pause while it arrives.

What the kernel did: it made the rulebook a ladder of dated versions instead of weather, so retroactive enforcement became arithmetic visible on the accusing entry itself; it made the statement of reasons a validity condition rather than a courtesy; it gave the frozen money an author, a rule, and a deadline whose breach counts; and it published the machine's misfire rates — the retroactivity percentage, the holds past clock — as standing, witnessed facts. What it did not do: it did not take the pen from the rulebook's author, price the fairness of any term, or bind the platforms that never signed. The confession industry dies not because sellers got braver but because disputes finally have material: two entries, two dates, and a subtraction anyone can perform.

verdict_plain: 'Suspended per Section 3' — a rule changed three weeks earlier without notice, money frozen with no author and no deadline, appeal by confession. Bound to the log, the rule change is a noticed, dated, expiring entry; the suspension must cite its version and facts; the hold has an owner and a clock; retroactivity becomes arithmetic. But the platform still writes the rules, and only statute makes the unwilling adopt.
protection: the rule that must name itself, the hold with a clock
protection_plain: Whether the amendment ladder and cited-demand discipline answer rule-by-decree: terms changing only on notice with expiry, suspensions citing rule version and facts, funds held only by an act with an author and release date, misfire rates published. The rulebook's author is untouched.

findings.observation: The amendment ladder's transplant is the scenario's cleanest win: once every terms change is a dated, noticed, diffed entry and every listing and enforcement carries its version, applying June's rule to March's listing stops being a seller's unprovable grievance and becomes a mismatch of two fields on the accusing entry itself. Petra's dispute needed one paragraph and no contrition because the wrongness was computable — and the aggregate generalises her case into a standing retroactivity rate. The expiry discipline adds the quieter gain: a term nobody will re-justify in two years lapses, so the rulebook accretes deliberately instead of geologically.

findings.protection: the statement of reasons as a validity condition
findings.observation: The P2B already obliges a statement of reasons; the Verge documented how little an obligation achieves when the reasons can be boilerplate and the forum is a parser. The kernel's move is to make the citation structural — an enforcement without rule version, named defect, and named subject cannot be validly logged, and under the settlement an unlogged enforcement is void. That kills the confession economy at its root: reinstatement consultants sold posture because disputes had no material, and a dispute that can cite two public entries at each other needs no posture. The sealed detection detail answers the gaming objection the same way the gig case did — committed now, examinable by the people with standing.

findings.protection: the money's silence gets a deadline
findings.observation: A frozen balance was the wrong's sharpest edge — the shop can be argued about; the wages are simply gone — and the old freeze had no author, no rule, and no date, the slow-walk signature the corpus met at FOI. As its own logged act the hold names all three, breaches of its clock are counted in public, and Petra's £9,180 came back fifty-one days early because the reversal executed along the record's references. The strain is what remains: thirty-one days is still the median winter a suspended seller's cashflow spends in escrow, the clock exists only because a settlement wrote one, and a lawful hold run to its full sixty days is a hardship the log dates precisely and relieves not at all.

findings.protection: the author of the rules is the counterparty
findings.observation: The structural ceiling the whole reform sits under: Kestrel drafts Section 3, and version 13 can lawfully outlaw Petra's business model on proper notice with a clean diff — the ladder makes decree legible and deliberate, never just. Whether a term is fair is the regulation's and company law's substance, refused on principle here (refusal 11). And the T2 shape gives the adoption gap its edge: the platform is not a neutral pipe that happens to be opaque — discretion is leverage, and the P2B's court-by-court enforcement is the slow march that stands where the depository case had no march at all. The CMA's dependence finding is the clock this strain runs on: exile does not wait for case law.
